segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]
[APPLAUSE]
MIRA LANE: Hi, everyone.

My name is Mira Lane, and I'm
the VP in Tech and Society
here at Google.

And today, we have the pleasure
of having Demis Hassabis, CEO

and Nobel Prize
winner, and Darren

Aronofsky, cinematic
pioneer joining us
for an amazing conversation.
Darren and Demis, come on stage.
[APPLAUSE, CHEERS]
MIRA LANE: All right.
Hello.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: Hi.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Hi.

MIRA LANE: OK, so
let's get started.

The two of you apparently
met 30 years ago.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: Yeah, yeah.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
Do you remember that?

DARREN ARONOFSKY: Well, he
remembers it better than me.
I had just finished "Pi."

We actually haven't
quite figured out how.

There's no evidence of it
out there in the world,
but he has a good memory of it.

DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, I think
we were trying to work out

backstage that we first met 30
years ago, early in our careers,

and we were on some
panel at a London event

with Neal Stephenson, as
well, who was our hero
at the time of science fiction.

It was a pretty cool panel,
but maybe we hallucinated it.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: First
film video game maker
and legendary sci-fi writer.
And then--

DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, and we
think it was called "The Future
of Storytelling."

So in a way, this is a nice full
circle here, being here today.
MIRA LANE: Well, you know what?

I looked it up, and
I couldn't find it.

And then I went
and asked Gemini.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: Oh.

MIRA LANE: And Gemini
said, yes, there

is evidence of you being on a
panel in 1999, 26 years ago,

discussing the future
of storytelling.

At the time, the
emerging technologies

were the internet, CD-ROMs,
interactive media like Flash,

and early forms of
interactive video games.

And so now we're talking
about AI 26 years ago.

DEMIS HASSABIS: That
dates us a little bit.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: The utmost
expert in CD-ROMs, everybody.
[LAUGHTER]

MIRA LANE: Maybe a few things
have happened since then.
All right, so let's get started.

Darren, you want to talk
to us about Primordial

Soup and the
strategic partnership

you have going on
with Google DeepMind?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
Well, when I first

started seeing some of
the stuff coming out

of the generative models
a couple of years ago,

I was really kind of
stunned, excited, inspired,

and I just started
to mess around.

And I knew that
it was definitely
going to impact storytelling.

I didn't know exactly how
long or how it would happen,

but I definitely
wanted to get involved.

So I started talking
to a lot of people

everywhere in the
business, and I hit Google.

DeepMind was definitely
an early interest

because I like the
leadership of Demis

and how it's always
pointing towards human use

and human benefit,
is what I feel like.

And I think storytelling
is of the same nature.
It's this incredible human art.
It might be bigger than art.

It's something that
defines us as a species.
MIRA LANE: Yep.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: And
there's so many things

threatening it for our
attention that I just

wanted to make sure
that storytellers stayed

on the front line
of storytelling

with these new
technologies and new tools.

MIRA LANE: And why did you
call it Primordial Soup?

DARREN ARONOFSKY: Because
it kind of felt like that.

It's like the whole
stability, nature of it,
and how it was emerging.

Things were just sort of
coming out of nothing.

My partner, Ari Handel, named it
that, and it just sort of stuck.

My other company
is called Protozoa,

so I'm into the early life forms
and the science fact stuff.

So our motto, kind of not
seriously, is, "Make soup,
not slop."

Because it feels like there's
a lot of slop coming out,

and it's really
grabbing our attention.

But we all have seen these
images, but how many have
actually stuck in our head?

You're like, whoa,
that's super cool,

and then you kind of
forget you ever saw it.

And I think what's missing from
it is emotional content, which
is storytelling over time.

And so I was like, OK,
how do we take this slop

and figure out how to
shape it and really empower

storytellers to tell stories
that we haven't told before?

MIRA LANE: And you're
going to be telling stories
through three films.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: Yeah.

MIRA LANE: You share a little
bit about that and the emerging

filmmakers you're going
to be working with?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
Yeah, well, I'm

interested in very veteran
storytellers and artists who

have been playing around and
have successful names, careers,

and worlds, and trying to
make sure that the tools
that are available to them.

So Demis has sort opened
up a lot of his team

to work with us to be
like, this is what we need.

This is how we're
going to tell stories.

And he's able to push his teams
to try to help us in that way.

But I'm also interested
in the AI-native kids.

Because I would have been one of
those kids if I was making "Pi"
today.

And I was in my 20s, it would
be me and five friends in a room

just sort of jamming, just
trying to figure out how to turn
this into a story.

But I could have used
the help of storytelling,

like how to actually shape
emotion and character.

So kind of both ends and
then meet in the middle.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

And Demis, you are one of those
authentic leaders that really

believes in the arts and
collaborating with artists,
musicians, filmmakers.

What does this
experience mean to you.

Why is it important
for Google DeepMind?

DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, I
think it's really critical.

And well, first of all, I wanted
to collaborate with Darren
on something for 30 years.
So this is our chance.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: A video game.

DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, maybe
a video game at some point.

But it's important for the whole
process of what we're building,

I think, here at Google and
at DeepMind, which-- we're
trying to create these tools.
And they're amazing.

And we want them to empower
the creative process

and help artists with
their creative process.

And what better
way of doing that?

You can't really do
that in isolation
just with the technology.
We're building that.

But we need input from
the creative community.

And what better way to
do that than collaborate

with some of the top
artists, filmmakers,

musicians in the
world like Darren,
who are also forward-thinking?

I think, always embracing
the possibilities

and the opportunities
of technology.
It's coming.
The change is coming.

It's sort of inevitable,
but we can shape it.

And I really
believe, and I think

we're both on the same page
with this, of shaping it
in a positive direction.

How can it help
with storytelling,

give these creative tools
and the things they create
some kind of soul, in a way?

I think that's where the
human creativity comes in.

And then these are just even
better tools than we had before.
That's how I see it.

And even the name we
called our tool, Flow,
I think it's to do with that.

I love that name, because we've
all experienced being in flow,

whether you're doing technical
creative work or artistic work.

It's the same kind of flow
state that we all know and love.

And I love these tools to
help us, in that flow state,
produce more.
And I think they can.

And I think these early
experiments are part of that.

The technology is
developing fast.

But we also need to
understand, what do artists
want from these tools?

Down to the technical
level, like camera controls
and other things--
what would be useful?

And we need to understand that
from the professionals working

in the industry and do that
very much as a collaboration.
And I'm a huge believer in that.

The technology is going
to impact everything.

We need to have wider
inputs into that, not just
the technologists.
MIRA LANE: Right.

Technological development is
inherently creative, as well.
Right?
This isn't--
DEMIS HASSABIS: Exactly.

MIRA LANE: There aren't hard
lines between these two fields.

DEMIS HASSABIS: And I know
you feel the same way in that
your technology career is.
And same with Darren, too.

And I learned that
early from games.

Because, in the '90s, when I
was making games as a teenager,

it was the golden era, in
some ways, of computer games,

exploration, this
whole new medium.

No one really knew
what to do with it.
Interactive media, what was it?

That was partly what our panel
was about in, whatever it was,
the late '90s.
MIRA LANE: 26 years ago.

DEMIS HASSABIS:
It was about, what

do you do in this interactive
media that's different from more
traditional media?

And I loved working
in games in that time

because it brought together
the best of technology

at the time--
graphics engines, AI,

even GPUs were
invented for games.

So all the cool stuff going
on in computers, in my view,

in the '80s and
'90s, was in games.

But you also worked
with artists, designers

to make the thing really
beautiful and fun.
So from very early on--

and most of my family actually
is on the creative and musician

side, so I'm the odd one out in
the sciences, as a scientist,

but I've always been around
in and around creatives.

And I love that combination
of technology and creativity.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

There is this
reinforcing cycle, I

think, between artists
and technologists,

where the artists are
pushing technologists,

and the technologists are
like, well, what about this?

And try this out
and back and forth.

And even in the
cultural ecosystem,

the stories that we hear, they
turn into the technologies
that we develop and vice versa.

And so how do you think
about that, Darren,

that kind of reinforcing cycle
between cinema and technology?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
It's interesting.

I often think about how
much the world we live in
was predicted by storytellers.
I'm a big fan of sci-fi.

The only thing missing from
"Star Trek" is the transporter,
which Dennis is working on.
MIRA LANE: Working on that.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: But everything
else kind of came to pass,
and it's all pretty wild.

I think, I agree, I think that
art can be found anywhere--

in technology, for sure, and
people coming up with it.

We've been collaborating on this
new film with all these people

at Google Labs and
throughout Google

that I don't know if there
are artists or technologists.
It's very hard to--

I don't think they know
how to define themselves.

I think it's a
bit of everything.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
Maybe, let's go under the hood.
Oh did you--

DEMIS HASSABIS:
Yeah, well, I'm just

going to add to
that that's why I'm

a huge fan of the Renaissance
Era and people like da Vinci.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

DEMIS HASSABIS:
Because back then,

they didn't, I don't think,
if you read biographies of da

Vinci, he didn't really, when
he was building his engines

and things like that versus
painting the "Mona Lisa,"

I think he just felt it was all
part of the creative process.
MIRA LANE: Right.

DEMIS HASSABIS: And
that's beautiful, I think.

And maybe we can get back
to that in this new era.

And I think that that's what
separates the good scientists

and the great scientists,
the good artists

and the great artists,
is their creativity.

Actually, on the science
side and technical side,
it's not the technical skills.

All good scientists
and engineers
have good technical skills.

But then it's the
creativity on top.

And I think it's very
similar, that process,

even though it's in
a different domain.

MIRA LANE: There are an
enormous number of artists
within Google DeepMind.
It is shocking.

For those of you that
might not realize this,

there are musicians, there
are storytellers, filmmakers.

And there is this kind of
inspirational back-and-forth
that goes on.

I know we'll actually get into
Eliza's film in a little bit.

But you'll hear about
some of the technicians

behind that, as well, and
the collaboration that's
been going on.

Demis, I was wondering if you
could share with the group

a little bit about just how
this technology is made.

What are some of the
considerations and approaches
that go into building Veo 3?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah.

MIRA LANE: How do you tune
for creativity or spontaneity,
and how do you think about that?

DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, I
think the reason we're

working on things like
Veo 3 is, obviously, we

want to build these
amazing creative tools.

Actually, the creativity
and the spontaneity

comes from the user and
the creator, I think.

In today's models, there's a lot
of skill in how you use and get
the most out of these models.
You know that.

You've been playing
around with them a lot,

and Darren, obviously,
and his team.

And you get very different
results from the same tool.

So it's a different
type of technical skill

that one has to
learn now and master.

But from a more of
an AGI perspective,

in terms of the
overall goal of AI,

I think it harks back to
what I talked yesterday

on the keynote
about world models.

In order for AI to
be truly general,

it's got to understand
the physical environment,

the world we live in,
the context we're in.

It can't just stay in symbolic
world of maths and language.

That's obviously
part of who we are.

But it's only a
part of what makes
the human world a civilization.

And one of the ways to
demonstrate that you understand

the world or the
system understands

some aspects of the world,
the physics of the world,

is can you generate
it accurately?
MIRA LANE: Yeah.

DEMIS HASSABIS: And
so if you can generate

those things and accurate
physics and light and gravity--
and we're not there yet.

But I mean, I think Veo is
incredible, what it can do,

and surprising even to those of
us working on it how accurate it
can be already.
But that's on the way to this.

And it's not there yet, but it's
on the way to this system that

really understands,
in a deep way

about the world, which we can
then use in many other domains.

And from an assistant
in your daily life

to cracking some areas
of science, maybe one day
some fundamental physics itself.

So I think it's a
key component of what
our AI systems need to do.

And then, over
time, you'll start

seeing a lot of what Veo
can do in main Gemini.

MIRA LANE: I think I would
push that a little bit,

too, because it not only
needs to be able to generate

the world, but with
art, sometimes we
need to hallucinate, as well.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah.

MIRA LANE: We don't always want
to follow gravity in the way

that you traditionally
experience it, right?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Exactly.
It's interesting.

I was asked this
earlier by a journalist
today about hallucination.

And in some contexts,
it's very useful.

So we call it
hallucination now, which

is a negative term when you're
information-seeking, obviously,
right?
So then you--

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
[LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE].
DEMIS HASSABIS: --want to then.

But of course, the same word,
the two sides of the coin,
is imagination.
It's which I studied for my PhD.
How does the brain do it?

And, obviously, vital
for creative processes,

you want sort of a
useful hallucination,
should we call it.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: I think
there's so much exciting things
in the hallucinations.

And like, I was talking
earlier with Demis

about the early HTML
artists that were just

sort of playing
around and messing

with what a website could be
and how exciting that was.

I'm so curious about the
art that's going to happen,

where the system falls
apart and breaks apart
and what that unveils.

And I think there'll
probably be people

that will be playing around with
early models for a long time.

A good friend of mine
did a poetry book,

I think, on ChatGPT,
two or three,

that gave just the first
two sentences of a poem
just to see what would come out.
And it was fascinating.

And it's kind of sad now
that it's so accurate.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: And
so there's something.

These early models for
video are super exciting.

MIRA LANE: And there's
something beautiful

even about the glitchiness
in the models, as well.

And that's something
we have to figure out.

How do we retain
that part of it?

DEMIS HASSABIS: Well,
I'm pretty sure we'll

be able to harness that
in an intentional way

where it's like
playful exploration

or imagination, probably guided
by the user or the creator.

And I think it could
be, definitely,

a useful component
of the system.

So we don't want to
lose that altogether--
MIRA LANE: Oh, exactly.

DEMIS HASSABIS: --in
the right circumstances.

MIRA LANE: Do you think
these models will exhibit
more innate creativity, Demis?

What does it take for
there to be like a move 37
in one of these models?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
Oh, interesting.

DEMIS HASSABIS:
Yeah, well, look, I

think move 37, the reason it
was so significant in AlphaGo

in the big Lee Sedol
match, world match,

it was probably
the first example

in a really heavily studied
area of human endeavor

that it came up with an
acknowledged truly novel idea.

Obviously, albeit within
the context of a game of Go,

but we have played Go
for hundreds of years
very seriously.

So it's been explored
very heavily.

And yet it was able
to find a totally sort
of novel, radical idea.

And that's what, for
me, I was waiting

to see before we started
all our science program.

Because it needs at
least that level,

and albeit relatively
constrained creativity,

to be able to help in scientific
problems like AlphaFold.

But there's another layer above
that which we're nowhere near

yet, I think, with
our AI systems,

where it's sort of-- we were
discussing this yesterday--

true out-of-the-box
thinking, true invention,

not just extrapolation
or interpolation.

And the examples I give there
is, could our systems come up

with general relativity
with the same information
Einstein had in 1900?
No, for sure not.

Could it have not just
come up with move 37,

could it have invented Go, a
game as elegant and as beautiful
as Go?
No.

So there's something
missing, still,

from what we're able
to do as human creators
from these systems.

But that doesn't
mean they already

can't do some
incremental creativity,
I would call it, like move--

like extrapolation of
what is currently known.

And our system, AlphaEvolve,
that we just released last week,

is another example of that,
using evolutionary techniques

to go a little bit beyond
what the current foundation
model or LLM knows.

So there's-- I think there's
going to be some interesting

exploration of hybrid systems,
where you have another system,

whether that's Monte Carlo tree
search or evolution that can get

your main model outside of
the scope of what it currently
knows.
MIRA LANE: Yep.

And they are pretty
constrained at the moment.

We were talking
about true artistry
does happen with constraints.

When you look at the
models right now,

Darren, they're not one where
you can type in a prompt

and have a beautiful film
just produced by itself.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: That's the
fear that's floating out there.
MIRA LANE: Exactly.
And that's not reality as well.

What does it look like
right now, actually?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
And I don't know

how near that reality is
for that level of control
and precision.

When you're-- and I asked Demis
this question a while ago,

which was, when
you're on a movie set,

you'll have hundreds of artists
working together that basically

have PhD levels of experience
to focus on this one moment
in time.

And then, at the core of that,
you have an actor who's truly
a one-in-a-million person.

They're there because they
have some type of thing

that they're able to express
that people all over the world

can experience and
understand in some way,

be it a little look
out of the corner
of their eye or a little smirk.
The entire world feels it.

And for a brief moment, in a
real three-dimensional space,

in real time, with
the restrictions

of the technology
that exist, something

happens where all these
artists come together
to create a moment.

That level of control is
not what this is about.

If anything, what I'm starting
to feel from it is it's

a form of collage,
where you're basically

asking for all these
different types of images.

And you're searching--
in the old way,

you'd be searching through
magazines and other places

to get images to cut them up and
move them and mix them around

together to bring
meaning to them.

So I'm more interested--
with storytelling,
it might be move 73, was it?
DEMIS HASSABIS: 37.

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
No, the next one.

DEMIS HASSABIS:
Oh, the other one?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
Yeah, the human one.
DEMIS HASSABIS: It was 79.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: 79?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah.

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
Move 79 is the one

that I think is happening a
lot, where there's basically

all this new stuff
coming at people

and how you're going
to fit it all together.

MIRA LANE: And you've talked
about the value of storytelling

and how that is even more
important now, given the desire
for everyone's attention.

Maybe talk a little bit about
why that's important for you
in particular.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: How do you
feel two hours after TikTok
or Instagram or YouTube--

how do you feel after that
two hours versus everyone

in this room has a two-hour
experience in a movie theater

where they completely remember
exactly where they were
and how they felt?

That's the power of
storytelling and movies.

And so I just-- we're
at a war for attention.

And storytelling is this
incredible art that connects us.

The fact that you can watch
a movie and be transported

into the shoes of a
seven-year-old girl in Iran
or an 80-year-old guy in Wales--
that's the magic of it.
It's not you.

It's not your face on
someone else's body.

It's just someone you've never
met before that you don't know.

Yet you completely
relate to them.

And everyone in the room,
when it's done well,
can relate to that person.

And that is so super important
to what makes us people.

And if we lose that and our
attention to that, it's--
I don't know the consequences.

MIRA LANE: How do you
feel about that, Demis?

DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, look, I
think I agree with-- actually,
with Darren.
And I-- on all those aspects.

And I think that's why
these collaborations are

so important at the
cutting edge of technology

and the cutting edge of
filmmaking, storytelling,
and artistic creativity.

Actually, we-- if you think
about all of our work we do,

I love collaborating with the
top domain experts in the area,

whether that's biology and
chemistry with AlphaFold.

We couldn't have done it
with just technologists.

We needed the domain experts,
whether it's filmmaking or music
or whether it's games playing.

And I have a huge respect for
all of those creative endeavors,

having been involved in
these creative endeavors
and-- at my start of my career.

And I think it's
really important

that we shape these
technologies in positive ways.

And you can only do that-- you
can't just do that in a silo
with just the technology.

You have to bring in the
experts in those domains

and really have some
humility about, yes, you're
building these cool things.

But you don't
necessarily know how
to use them in the right way.

That's what people like
Darren are here for.

And then it's a learning--
it's a joint learning process.
We're both exploring.

None of us know what's going
to happen in 5, 10 years.

But we can definitely help
shape it with our platforms

and I think by collaborating
in this really deep way.
And I think it's beautiful.

And I think that's the way we'll
win this war, as Darren talked

about, of we're
providing these tools,

but the tools can be used
for good or for bad--

and in general, but also
in the artistic sense.

And I hope that through
these kind of collaborations,

we can power up the
good, these wholesome

experiences, these meaningful,
delightful experiences,

versus the fast food version of
it, which is happening, anyway,
by the way, without AI.

It's not like TikTok, Instagram,
and these other things
are-- it's happening, anyway.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: But
the tools are making
them more attention-grabbing.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Of course.

But it could be-- it
could-- but it's also

allowing the good side of
it to potentially produce
more output as well.

So it's going to be an
interesting-- we'll see.

And the thing is the way I look
at it is the change is coming.

So you can't-- like with
all technology leaps,

there's so many amazing
opportunities here.

The horse has
bolted, if you like.
It's not going back in the barn.
But we can shape it from here.
And that's what we have to do.

And that's the history of
technology and creativity,

I think, from the
very beginning--

is how do we-- we're talking
about hunter-gatherers

with telling stories
around the fire and then
the cave paintings.
It's the very beginning.

If you think of fire as
the first technology,
they're intertwined.

And I think that it's
the same here today.

And even our story,
of how we met

each other, and then back
now, future of storytelling--
we're in the past.
And now we're in the now.

And then there's a
future version of that.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: I always think
it's always been a technology
business, filmmaking.
MIRA LANE: Has, yeah.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: The
camera and the projector

came before
storytelling found it.

The early shots were of
their factory workers.

And then we all know
what happened with sound,

Al Jolson saying "You
ain't heard nothing yet,"

unleashing musicals and a
whole new way of communicating
to the masses.
And then, of course, color--

just think about the
storytelling use of color
in "The Wizard of Oz."

And then VFX-- and when
I was a kid, it was--

Spielberg and Lucas had
the keys to the kingdom.

And now pretty much
every filmmaker

is using all types of VFX in
all different types of ways,
ways that you don't even notice.

And the question-- this is
a radical new technology
that's very game-changing.

And no one, really, can
understand exactly how
it's going to impact.

But it's definitely
going to have impact.

And to say it's not
there, that-- there

will be many filmmakers, I
think, that will do that,
and I will, too.

I have a movie coming
out this summer.

And I continue to--
will make those.

But the tools are just very,
very helpful and powerful.
And we just have to--

we have to learn
to work with them.

MIRA LANE: What do you think
about the democratization

of this type of
technology, the types
of stories that might come out?
What do you want to see happen?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
That's always exciting.

When I teach, I'm always
telling kids all over the world,

go tell a story that means
something to you in your life.

The more personal it can
be, as long as it's--

as long as you feel it's
going to move your mom
and dad and your friends--

means it's probably going to
move people all over the earth
if you do it well.

So I do think that, as these
tools become more and more

available, they just give more
opportunities for storytellers
around the world.
It's similar, though.

It's like-- Francis
Ford Coppola had

a quote, which I
can't quote in full,

about a girl with a video camera
making the next great film when

video cameras first
started to come around.

And there's definitely going to
be films that we've never done.

But I still think storytelling
takes a lot of craft,

a lot of patience,
a lot of work.

And there will always be
collaboration involved with it.

It's never going to be a person
with some prompts on a computer.

It's always going to be a
team of people coming together
to figure it out.

MIRA LANE: Maybe on
that note, why don't we

dig into the-- one of the
films that you guys are working
on right now with Eliza McNitt?

We'll pull you on
stage in just a moment.

But share with the
audience a little bit

about what that
process has been like

and that back-and-forth
that's been

going on with the technologists,
what it's been actually
like working with the tools.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: Well,
Eliza will fill us in more.
But it's been a wild experience.

It's really good to keep this
old brain with fresh new stuff.

So it's been wild just to see
everything that's going on
and is possible.

What blew my mind
is, at the beginning,
was, you have these models.

And they really, actually,
don't know what they do.

And so they were building tools
to prod these models to see
what it will do.

And that blew my
mind as an idea--

that there's so many
possibilities in there.

But this has been a huge
team from Primordial Soup

and from Google and
Google DeepMind of--

we've had as many
workers on this

as you would have
on a normal movie.

It was really important
to Eliza to have
a traditional human-based shoot.
She'll talk a lot about that.
But there's real actors.

And we made a deal with SAG
and with the unions to do that.
And it was very interesting.
There's a baby in this.

It's about a birth,
as you'll see--
not giving away too much.
But it's really--
I've worked-- I've had babies.

I've [? eaten ?] babies
[? and ?] mother.

So have I have experience
with how hard it
is to have babies on set.

And it's also-- it's ethically a
little questionable because it's
someone else's child.

And of course, they're
not sentient enough
to make that decision.

So you're making a
decision for them.

So we actually-- the
Google DeepMind team

did some incredible newborn and
embryo forms that are just wild.

So it was incredible
things that are

just impossible to photograph
that were done, and not--

in ways that could
be done with VFX,

but they were done
in ways to see if--

how-- it's amazing how easy to
bring this stuff to life is.

MIRA LANE: You think we
can take greater risks?

We've talked about
risk-taking and how

this might increase
the types of stories

that you might not
have told in the past.
DARREN ARONOFSKY: Absolutely.

You will learn so much
by generating ideas

and seeing what works
and doesn't work
and putting them together.

It's like storyboarding
on the next level.
MIRA LANE: Yeah.
Yep.

So why don't we
play Eliza's video?
Eliza will bring you on stage.

And then we'll talk a
little bit about what

it took to create your video,
which is still in progress,
actually.
[AUDIO PLAYBACK]

- Film has always been
this deeply human act

of connecting people with
each other's stories.

And it has the ability to
rip us out of our experience
and take us on another journey.
I don't think that ever changes.

- This is a story that
traverses the entire history
of the universe.

But it's really
about a mom and what

happens when her child is
born with a hole in her heart.

We filmed really
emotional performances,

but then generated video we
could never capture otherwise.

I want the baby to be
holding the mother's finger.

- Just the bliss
of the two of them?
- Yeah.

- Veo is a generative
video model.

But to me, it's another
lens through which

I get to imagine the
universe around me.

- It's been incredibly
interesting to see

the strengths of these models,
the limits of the models,

and try to shape them to
make storytelling tools.

- To be honest, I never
thought about telling the story
of the day I was born.
But here we are.
[BEEPING]
[SOFT MUSIC]

- This was supposed
to be a checkup.
- Crash, C-section.
- Baby's in distress.
- We need help here!
- We need to go.
- Is my baby going to be OK?

- We're going to do
everything we can.
- Am I going to be OK?

- For every creature that came
before you, from every star that
died so that you could begin--
[BREATHING]
[MUSIC PLAYING]
[END PLAYBACK]
DEMIS HASSABIS: Very cool.
[APPLAUSE]
ELIZA MCNITT: Hello.
MIRA LANE: Eliza, welcome.
ELIZA MCNITT: Thank you.

MIRA LANE: So I want to
introduce you really briefly
for the audience.

Eliza is a visionary
writer and director

who masterfully blends
science and the arts

to tell profound stories
about humanity's connection
to the cosmos.

Her groundbreaking works,
such as "Sphere" and "Astra,"

has captivated audiences
and critics alike,

earning accolades at prestigious
festivals like Sundance
and Venice.

So Eliza, maybe tell
everybody a little bit

about your film, the
inspiration behind it.

And get us behind the
scenes a little bit.
ELIZA MCNITT: Sure.

So the day that I was
born, I nearly died.

And my mom went into the
hospital for a routine checkup

and was told that she had to
have an emergency C-section.

And all of a sudden,
her life and my life
were completely in question.

And so this is the
story of the day

that my mother brought
me into the universe.
Thank you.
[APPLAUSE]

MIRA LANE: That is such
a personal story, Eliza.
ELIZA MCNITT: Yes.

MIRA LANE: What has
this process been like?

And a lot of the folks
you've been working with

are actually in the
audience as well.

So thank you to everybody who's
been working on this project.
What's it been like?

ELIZA MCNITT: Well, I think,
for me, ultimately, this

is a story about the
ferocity of a mother's love.

And it's a really
personal story.

And I think when I did
"Spheres" with Darren,

I really hid myself behind
this grand image of the cosmos.

And I felt with this
was an opportunity

to really tear out a
literal piece of my heart
and throw it into the universe.

So I'm quite
excited and nervous.

And it's just been just
the-- just the experience

of a lifetime to work with
the team at the Creative Lab

and with the amazing group of
people that we have who are
both--
they are artists.

And it's just been the
most creative environment

that I could have imagined
working in as a filmmaker.

So I've been super
inspired and pushed

every day beyond what I
thought what was possible.

MIRA LANE: And there's a lot of
technicians behind the scenes,
as well, right?

ELIZA MCNITT: Yes, a lot
of-- so many technicians.

There's about 15
generators who are--

and artists who are working on
creating all the images that you

just saw, who are just
dedicated to that.

And we just counted the credits
before I got on stage here.

And there are over 165 people
who have been working tirelessly
on this film.
So it's pretty remarkable.
[APPLAUSE]

But I have to say, as
far as the process--

and thank you, Demis,
for this-- there's

been-- in the process
of generating,

there's a lot of nightmares
that come out of it.

So it's been a very
interesting process

to create and see what comes
out when you embrace that chaos.

MIRA LANE: And you
have a hybrid approach.
ELIZA MCNITT: Yes.

MIRA LANE: So some of this
is footage that's been shot.

It's not all
AI-generated film, right?
ELIZA MCNITT: Yeah, absolutely.

For me, one major boundary
for me, as a filmmaker is--

and I think Darren said
it so beautifully-- it's

about capturing that moment with
humans and a performance that

can only be captured
by humanity.

And so that, for me, was so
crucial to create with this.

So we had a full live-action
shoot with an incredible actress

named Audrey Corsa,
who just cosmically has
the same name as my own mother.
So it was all really beautiful.

MIRA LANE: And Darren,
you brought this up.

But there's some scenes
with a baby, baby Eliza,
that were AI-generated.
So what was that process like?

And walk us through
generating a baby.

ELIZA MCNITT: So as Darren
said, working with a baby--
it's super unethical.

And with this film, we
want our baby to consent.

So the baby is actually
me in this film.

And we used my late
father's images from the day

that I was born to train a
style to create me as a newborn
and put that in the film.

I will say, I think it added
a few pounds to me as a baby.
But I still look adorable.

So that's been this incredibly
remarkable part of the process.

And another thing, too, is
my father was a photographer.

And his images have
always deeply inspired me.

He used to climb up in
helicopters and planes

and hang out of them and take
photos of boats from the sky.

And he had incredible film
stock and a beautiful style.

And so we've used
something called

a LoRA to train a style
that we could only

do on images that are
copyrighted that we own

to create a touch of my father's
vision to also implement

in the film, as well, so
that it's not just about me

and my mom, but it also has a
piece of my dad's heart as well.

MIRA LANE: And Demis,
I thought maybe

we could talk to the audience
a little bit about how

these models work
with LoRAs and the way

that people can bring in their
footage and their content

so that it can be a little
bit more specific to the style
of what they have.

DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, it's
amazing what Eliza's done,
and a very moving story.
And actually, I think--

I didn't know that that's
what you were going to do.

But it's actually
personal connection for me

because my mom's mom died
giving birth to my mom.

So my mom had a really
hard start in life.

So it's very moving for
me to see that, too.

And I think it's amazing, which
is this collage idea that Darren

was talking about, where we
have Whisk and other tools where

you can bring in images and
other things that you want.

And then it will shift
the generation process
to be more in that style.

So it's amazing
use case, actually,

of your childhood,
your baby pictures.
And then it becomes--
it looks like you.
And I think we'll refine--

actually, you pushing the
technology to the limits

will allow us to refine
those tools so that they'll

be even more consistent
over frames and over time,

and more useful
to you, hopefully.

MIRA LANE: So what do you
think is the next mountain

range, Demis, in terms
of these Veo models?

So we announced this
week Veo with sound.

So where do you think
these models are going?

DEMIS HASSABIS: Well,
I think it's incredible

that the-- the speed at
which they're improving.
I think the sound--

actually, I'm surprised
how much of a difference
it makes to these videos.

It's hard to imagine now they're
not being generated with sound
once you see Veo 3, actually.
It's pretty astounding.

And I think the internet's
already normalized that now.

I think the next step is
what we discussed earlier--

a world model, more
consistency over time--
it's still only 8 seconds--

and even higher fidelity
and those things--

so just more accuracy about
a wider range of things.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: I want him to
put a hallucination button in.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, we will--
yes, exactly.

Once we can stop it
doing it unintentionally,

we can put a button back in
where you can intentionally

make it intentional
hallucination maybe toward--

with some extra guidance,
like these are the sorts
of things I want you to--
DARREN ARONOFSKY: Yes.
MIRA LANE: New feature request.

Let's continue this.

We're on a panel not

but maybe a few years from now.

But we're on a panel talking

of technology and storytelling.

What does that world
look like in a few years?

DARREN ARONOFSKY:
Well, I think we'll

was just like CD-ROMs.
[LAUGHTER]

You're like, wow,
how wrong we were.

DEMIS HASSABIS: Definitely 26

DARREN ARONOFSKY: We just

DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, exactly.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: You

DEMIS HASSABIS: What's the
specific question, Mira,
you wanted to ask?

MIRA LANE: What do you
imagine the world will
look like in a few years?

And Darren, you can't
skip out on this question,

curious about what

new forms of
storytelling might exist.

You guys are pushing
the boundary.

It's not necessarily going
to be linear storytelling.

There might be other
ways that people are
experimenting with these tools.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: What's
exciting to me about "Ancestra,"

just to talk about that project
and just hearing Eliza talk,

it's like, she probably would
have never gone to this project
without this technology.
ELIZA MCNITT: Absolutely.

DARREN ARONOFSKY: And it's an
incredibly personal technology.
And I'm not going to push her.

But I can only
imagine what it might

have meant for
her and her family
to do something like this.
And I think that's--

and when I-- when we
decided, Primordial Soup,

to work with Eliza, we didn't
know she would lean into that.

We just knew she was this

could connect emotional stories
and also work with technology.

And so we were just excited
to see what happened.

And that she stumbled

for this experiment--

I think that's-- that's
kind of-- show what's going

to happen, very
personal stories.
ELIZA MCNITT: Definitely.

I think on that point, it's
like, I think the future

that this offers us
is the opportunity

to really connect with
our families in ways
that we didn't anticipate.

And through this project
and this process,

it's just opened up a whole
new relationship with my mom

and finding out stories that
I never knew about my birth.

And we had the entire
group at the Creative Lab--

Ben Wiley and [? Hobby ?] and

And this is not a

And I think, what's amazing

without having to spend tons
of money that's not accessible.

So I hope that
that's the future.

DEMIS HASSABIS: Maybe two

this is a wonderful

And you can see it just--
the beginnings of it.

I think, hopefully, we can

I think this is just

So maybe in 10 years' time,

And hopefully, we've

in terms of creating nourishing

And that's what we want to do.

And then on the

Well, the next

I'm going to eventually

And Genie 2 is one example.

And now we're combining

So then one can imagine you

What can you do with that, both

DARREN ARONOFSKY: Storytelling.

DEMIS HASSABIS: --and
